title: UC Davis — Alfalfa for Livestock
notes: Alfalfa — yield_kg_ha: Source for typical yield and agronomic characteristics.
Alfalfa — water_per_kg: Source for water use as livestock feed.
Alfalfa — human_food flag: Confirmed primarily a livestock feed crop (human_food = 0).

title: Wikipedia — List of Animals by Number of Neurons

Broiler chicken and layer hen — neuron_count: ~221 million neurons in domestic chicken.
Pig — neuron_count: ~2.23 billion neurons.
Tuna — neuron_count: ~30 million neurons.

title: Our World in Data — Tomato Yields

title: Our World in Data — Rice Yields

title: Mekonnen & Hoekstra — Water Footprint of Crops (Report 47)
notes: General: Comprehensive peer-reviewed global crop water footprint database; primary source for most water_per_kg values.
Almonds — water_per_kg: 13,080 L/kg; averaged with ScienceDirect California study (source 7, 10,240 L/kg) → final 11,660 L/kg.

Lentils — water_per_kg: 1,250 L/kg (global weighted average; Report 47 Table A1). Previous dataset value of 5,874 L/kg was a clear error (4.7× too high), corrected 2026-05-21. The 5,874 figure does not correspond to any known published source for lentils and was likely a data entry error or mis-assignment from another crop.

Rice — water_per_kg: Report 47 Table A1 gives 1,673 L/kg for paddy rice. Dataset uses 2,500 L/kg which reflects milled white rice basis: 1,673 ÷ 0.65 milling yield ≈ 2,574 L/kg. This is consistent with Chapagain & Hoekstra (2011) who report 2,291–2,500 L/kg for white rice. The 2,500 value is accepted as correct for the milled-rice basis used throughout the dataset.

Lettuce — water_per_kg: 130 L/kg used (sourced from WFN online crop database). Report 47 Table A1 reports 237 L/kg. The discrepancy reflects different methodologies: Report 47 uses a global production-weighted virtual water model; the WFN online calculator (130 L/kg) applies simplified blue+green accounting. Neither is clearly wrong; the lower figure may reflect the dominant role of intensive irrigation-efficient greenhouse production (particularly in China). Value flagged but not corrected — difference is within methodological range.

Cucumber — water_per_kg: 240 L/kg used. Report 47 Table A1 reports 353 L/kg. Dataset figure is 32% lower than the peer-reviewed value. The discrepancy may reflect greenhouse production dominance (China, Netherlands) which achieves higher yields per m³. Value is in a plausible range; flagged but not corrected.
Walnuts — water_per_kg: 7,744 L/kg; averaged with ScienceDirect tree nuts study (source 34, 9,279 L/kg) → final 8,512 L/kg.
Pistachios — water_per_kg: 10,697 L/kg; averaged with ScienceDirect tree nuts study (source 34, 11,356 L/kg) → final 11,027 L/kg.
Hazelnuts — water_per_kg: 9,807 L/kg; averaged with ScienceDirect tree nuts study (source 34, 10,511 L/kg) → final 10,159 L/kg.

Peanuts — water_per_kg: 3,740 L/kg; averaged with secondary sources → final 3,537 L/kg. Report 47 gives 2,782 L/kg for in-shell peanuts; 3,740 reflects shelled/edible kernel basis after accounting for ~74% kernel recovery. Difference is within expected range for basis discrepancy.
Sweet potato — water_per_kg: 461 L/kg (global weighted average, primarily rain-fed tropical production).
Chickpeas — water_per_kg: 4,325 L/kg (global weighted average, includes irrigation and green water; high due to arid growing regions).
Black beans — water_per_kg: 5,053 L/kg (global weighted average; Phaseolus vulgaris dry beans; includes Brazil and Mexico as major producers; verified against Report 47 Table A1).
Quinoa — water_per_kg: 3,337 L/kg (global weighted average; South American Andean production, largely rain-fed).
Spinach — water_per_kg: 305 L/kg (global average; short-cycle leafy crop with low total water input).
Avocado — water_per_kg: 1,981 L/kg (global average; intensive irrigation in arid growing regions, e.g., Chile, Mexico).
Sunflower seeds — water_per_kg: 3,366 L/kg (global average; semi-arid production in EU, Russia, Argentina).
Flaxseed — water_per_kg: 3,671 L/kg (global average; includes Canada and Russia as main producers).
Barley — water_per_kg: 1,423 L/kg (global average; temperate grain, primarily rain-fed).
Mango — water_per_kg: 686 L/kg (global average; tropical tree crop, mixed rain-fed and irrigated).
Orange — water_per_kg: 460 L/kg (global average; largely irrigated Mediterranean and subtropical production).
Garlic — water_per_kg: 656 L/kg (global average; irrigated production in China, India, US).
Kale — water_per_kg: 237 L/kg (global average; cool-season leafy brassica, lower water demand than most crops).
Olive oil — water_per_kg: 14,430 L/kg (global average for olive oil; raw olives ~3,025 L/kg with ~5× oil extraction factor; high due to Mediterranean rain-fed vs irrigated production mix).
Coconut — water_per_kg: 2,500 L/kg (estimate; limited direct data in Report 47; derived from coconut palm water use literature and tropical plantation studies; confidence 2).

title: Pig333 — Feed Conversion Ratio in Pigs
notes: Pig — FCR: ~2.8 kg feed per kg live weight gain.
Pig — dressing percentage: ~73%, used to calculate kg feed per kg dressed pork.

title: Seafish — Atlantic Salmon Feed Profile
notes: Farmed salmon — FCR: ~1.15 kg feed per kg live weight gain.
Farmed salmon — dressing percentage: ~90%, used to calculate kg feed per kg dressed fillet.
Farmed salmon — feed composition: Soy ~19%, wheat ~10% of diet by weight.

title: Our World in Data — Crop Yields
notes: Cucumber — yield_kg_ha: FAO global average used for yield value.
Lettuce — yield_kg_ha: FAO global average used for yield value.
Potato — yield_kg_ha: FAO global average used for yield value.
Apple — yield_kg_ha: FAO global average used for yield value.
Banana — yield_kg_ha: FAO global average used for yield value.
Lentils — yield_kg_ha: FAO global average used for yield value.
Oats — yield_kg_ha: FAO global average used for yield value.
Broccoli — yield_kg_ha: FAO global average used for yield value.
Sweet potato — yield_kg_ha: 11,000 kg/ha (tropical and subtropical production; China dominates global output at ~55% of supply; FAO 2020–2022 average).
Chickpeas — yield_kg_ha: 1,000 kg/ha (low-input arid legume; global average depressed by dryland Indian subcontinent production; FAO 2020–2022).
Black beans — yield_kg_ha: 850 kg/ha (global average; Brazilian and Mexican production; low-input systems; FAO 2020–2022).
Quinoa — yield_kg_ha: 1,700 kg/ha (Andean production; yield has improved with expansion to new growing regions; FAO 2020–2022).
Spinach — yield_kg_ha: 20,000 kg/ha (intensive irrigated vegetable production; China accounts for ~90% of global spinach output; FAO 2020–2022).
Avocado — yield_kg_ha: 11,000 kg/ha (FAO global average; modern commercial orchards in Mexico, Peru, Chile; 2020–2022).
Sunflower seeds — yield_kg_ha: 2,000 kg/ha (global average across Russia, Ukraine, EU production zones; FAO 2020–2022).
Flaxseed — yield_kg_ha: 1,300 kg/ha (global average; Canada, Kazakhstan, Russia as top producers; FAO 2020–2022).
Barley — yield_kg_ha: 3,100 kg/ha (global average; EU, Russia, Australia main producers; FAO 2020–2022).
Mango — yield_kg_ha: 8,000 kg/ha (global average; India, China, Thailand as top producers; FAO 2020–2022).
Orange — yield_kg_ha: 24,000 kg/ha (global average; Brazil, US, EU as major producers; FAO 2020–2022).
Garlic — yield_kg_ha: 18,000 kg/ha (global average; China dominates with ~75% of world production; FAO 2020–2022).
Kale — yield_kg_ha: 15,000 kg/ha (estimate derived from brassica family yields; FAO does not report kale separately; value consistent with broccoli/cabbage leaf yields; confidence 3).
Olive oil — yield_kg_ha: 400 kg/ha (oil-equivalent yield; global average including extensive traditional orchards and modern intensive systems; FAO olive data adjusted for oil extraction).
Coconut — yield_kg_ha: 5,600 kg/ha (FAO global average for coconut meat equivalent; Philippines, Indonesia, India as top producers; 2020–2022).

title: ScienceDirect — Tree Nuts and Sustainable Water Use (EAT-Lancet)
notes: Cashews — water_per_kg: 45,914 L/kg (used directly; no conflicting peer-reviewed source found).

Cashews — water_per_kg note: The 45,914 L/kg figure is believed to reflect a shell-basis water footprint (accounting for ~20–25% kernel recovery from the raw nut). All other nuts in this dataset use kernel-basis figures. The source 34 cashew value is therefore not comparable and has been replaced with the Mekonnen & Hoekstra kernel-basis estimate of 9,280 L/kg (source 30). Original value: 45,914 L/kg.
Walnuts — water_per_kg: 9,279 L/kg; averaged with Mekonnen & Hoekstra (source 30, 7,744 L/kg) → final 8,512 L/kg.
Pistachios — water_per_kg: 11,356 L/kg; averaged with Mekonnen & Hoekstra (source 30, 10,697 L/kg) → final 11,027 L/kg.
Hazelnuts — water_per_kg: 10,511 L/kg; averaged with Mekonnen & Hoekstra (source 30, 9,807 L/kg) → final 10,159 L/kg.

title: INC — Nuts & Dried Fruits Statistical Yearbook 2024
notes: Walnuts — yield_kg_ha: Industry yield data.
Cashews — yield_kg_ha: Industry yield data.
Pistachios — yield_kg_ha: Industry yield data.
Pecans — yield_kg_ha: Industry yield data.
Hazelnuts — yield_kg_ha: Industry yield data.
Macadamia nuts — yield_kg_ha: Industry yield data.
Brazil nuts — yield_kg_ha: Industry yield data.

title: Champrix — Feed Conversion Ratio in Layer Hens
notes: Layer hen — FCR (productive period only): ~2.2 kg feed per kg eggs during the laying phase.
Layer hen — FCR note: This is the laying-phase baseline only; see source 38 for the full lifetime calculation that also includes chick and pullet phases.

title: Alberta Milk — Feed Required per Litre of Milk
notes: Dairy cow — feed ratio (productive period only): ~1.35 kg feed per kg milk during active lactation.
Dairy cow — feed note: This is the lactation-phase baseline only; see source 39 for the full lifetime calculation that also includes dry periods and heifer rearing.

title: The Poultry Site — Layer Hen Feed and Production Over Lifetime
notes: Layer hen — lifetime feed: ~2 kg chick phase + ~9 kg pullet rearing + ~58 kg laying phase = ~69 kg total feed per bird.
Layer hen — lifetime egg output: ~300 eggs ≈ 17.1 kg eggs per bird over productive life.
Layer hen — lifetime FCR: 69 ÷ 17.1 ≈ 4.04 kg feed per kg eggs.
Layer hen — feed split applied: 60% corn / 20% soy → corn 2.42 kg, soy 0.81 kg per kg eggs.

title: Dairy Global — Feed Efficiency in Dairy Cows
notes: Dairy cow — lifetime feed breakdown: ~36,450 kg lactation feed (1.35 kg/kg milk × 27,000 kg milk) + ~2,700 kg dry-period feed (3 periods × 60 days × 15 kg/day) + ~5,840 kg heifer rearing (730 days × 8 kg DM/day) = ~44,990 kg total.
Dairy cow — lifetime milk output: ~27,000 kg per cow over productive life.
Dairy cow — lifetime FCR: 44,990 ÷ 27,000 ≈ 1.67 kg feed per kg milk.
Dairy cow — feed split applied: 55% corn / 25% alfalfa / 10% soy / 7% grass hay → corn 0.92, alfalfa 0.42, soy 0.17, hay 0.12 kg per kg milk.

title: EA Forum — Neuron Count Measures May Underweight Suffering in Farmed Fish
notes: Atlantic salmon — neuron_count: value used is 130,000,000 (130 million), derived as follows.

The commonly cited figure of 10 million neurons for salmon originates from an empirical count of a 0.75g zebrafish. Applying this to a ~3,000g Atlantic salmon is biologically indefensible — the figure does not scale with body or brain size.

Bruns & Davies (2022) ran a Monte Carlo simulation estimating 8–64 million neurons for salmon-sized fish, but this range was derived from data on fish under 4g and extrapolated across a >750× weight gap, so the uncertainty is substantial.

A scaling-adjusted estimate: applying Invincible Wellbeing's vertebrate curve-fitting formula to a 3,000g salmon yields ~1.9 billion neurons, but fish brains are roughly 1/15th the relative size of mammals and birds at equivalent body weight. Dividing by 15 gives ~130 million, which is the value used here.

All estimates carry significant uncertainty. The 130M figure is the most biologically grounded available estimate given the absence of direct neurological counts for Atlantic salmon at commercial harvest size.

title: Wikipedia — Domestic Turkey
notes: Turkey — weight_kg: Commercial tom turkeys at slaughter typically weigh 11–13 kg live weight; hens 6–8 kg. Value of 10 kg used as a weighted average reflecting the commercial flock mix.

Turkey — neuron_count: No direct empirical neuronal count for turkeys is available in the literature. As a closely related galliform to the domestic chicken, an estimate of ~262 million neurons is applied, slightly above the 221 million reported for chickens (Herculano-Houzel methodology), consistent with turkey brain mass being moderately larger than chicken brain mass at commercial slaughter size.

title: The Poultry Site — Turkey Production and Feed Conversion
notes: Turkey — FCR: Commercial turkeys achieve ~2.5–2.7 kg feed per kg live weight gain; value of 2.6 used.
Turkey — dressing percentage: ~80% for commercial toms, used to convert FCR to kg feed per kg dressed meat.
Turkey — feed composition: Similar to broiler chicken — corn 55–60%, soybean meal 20–25%, wheat 8–12% of diet by weight.
Turkey — feed split applied: corn 60% × 2.6 FCR / 0.80 dressing = 1.95 kg corn/kg meat; soy 22% = 0.71 kg; wheat 10% = 0.33 kg per kg turkey meat.

title: PMC — Nervous Systems of Decapod Crustaceans
notes: Shrimp — neuron_count: Decapod crustaceans (including penaeoid shrimp) have been estimated at approximately 100,000–1,000,000 neurons in the central nervous system, with most commercial penaeid shrimp species falling near the lower end (~100,000–500,000). A value of 1,000,000 is used here as a conservative upper bound for commercial-sized shrimp (~15–20g), consistent with estimates for small decapods in the peer-reviewed literature.

title: WWF — Bycatch Overview
notes: Shrimp trawling — bycatch_amount: Gulf of Mexico and Southeast US shrimp trawl fisheries have historically reported bycatch-to-shrimp ratios of 3–10 kg discarded finfish per kg shrimp landed, with a commonly cited average of ~5.7 kg bycatch per kg shrimp.

Shrimp trawling — bycatch composition: Bycatch consists predominantly of small demersal and pelagic finfish, sea turtles (incidental), and invertebrates. Finfish bycatch is represented here using sardines (animal_id=10) as a proxy for small pelagic and demersal finfish.

Shrimp — wild vs. farmed: Approximately 55% of global shrimp production is wild-caught (predominantly trawl). The bycatch values apply to the wild-caught fraction. Farmed shrimp contribute no trawl bycatch but have separate feed and environmental impacts.

title: Wikipedia — Sardine
notes: Sardines — weight_kg: Commercial sardines (Sardina pilchardus, Sardinops sagax) at harvest typically weigh 50–150g; a value of 0.1 kg (100g) is used as representative.

Sardines — neuron_count: No direct empirical neuronal count for sardines exists in the peer-reviewed literature. As small teleost fish with brain structure comparable to other clupeoids, an estimate of ~10 million neurons is applied, consistent with figures used for small pelagic fish of similar body size (e.g., reference values for small fish in the range of 5–30 million neurons). This carries significant uncertainty.

title: FAO — The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture 2018
notes: Sardines — bycatch: Sardines are harvested primarily by purse seine, which is a highly selective method with bycatch rates generally below 5–10% of total catch weight, substantially lower than demersal trawl fisheries.

Sardines — bycatch_amount: A value of 0.1 kg bycatch per kg sardines landed is used, reflecting the low but non-zero incidental catch typical of small pelagic purse seine operations (primarily other small schooling fish and occasional juvenile finfish).

Sardines — bycatch_animal_id: Bycatch represented using tuna (animal_id=5) as a proxy for larger incidental finfish catch in pelagic fisheries, though actual bycatch species composition varies by region and season.

title: Wikipedia — Sheep
notes: Lamb — weight_kg: Commercial lambs are typically slaughtered at 40–55 kg live weight; a value of 45 kg is used.

Lamb — neuron_count: Sheep have been estimated at approximately 1.2 billion neurons based on isotropic fractionator data for ovine brain (Herculano-Houzel methodology). Sheep brain mass (~140g) is substantially smaller than cattle (~500g), consistent with the proportionally lower neuron count relative to beef cattle (~3 billion).

title: MLA — Sheep Feed and Nutrition
notes: Lamb — FCR: Commercial lambs on feedlot finishing achieve ~4.5–5.5 kg feed per kg live weight gain; value of 5.0 used for feedlot lamb.
Lamb — dressing percentage: ~50% for commercial lamb, used to convert FCR to kg feed per kg dressed meat.
Lamb — feed composition: Finishing rations typically include corn 40–50%, soy 10–15%, grass hay 20–30%, alfalfa 10–20% by weight.
Lamb — feed split applied: corn 45% × 5.0 FCR / 0.50 dressing = 4.5 kg corn/kg meat; soy 12% = 1.2 kg; hay 28% = 2.8 kg; alfalfa 15% = 1.5 kg per kg lamb meat.

title: FAO — Farmed Whiteleg Shrimp (Penaeus vannamei)
notes: Farmed shrimp — feed composition: Commercial shrimp feeds typically contain soybean meal 25–35% and wheat 15–25% as primary plant-based ingredients; fishmeal provides 10–25% (animal origin, not tracked here).
Farmed shrimp — FCR: Modern semi-intensive and intensive penaeid shrimp aquaculture achieves FCR of ~1.3–1.6 kg feed per kg live shrimp; value of 1.5 used.
Farmed shrimp — dressing percentage: ~67% (heads-on whole shrimp converted to headless); plant feed values computed per kg headless shrimp.
Farmed shrimp — feed split applied: soy 30% × 1.5 / 0.67 = 0.67 kg soy/kg shrimp; wheat 20% × 1.5 / 0.67 = 0.45 kg wheat/kg shrimp. Applies to farmed fraction (~45% of global supply) only.

title: Fantke et al. (2014) — Ecotoxicological Characterization of Pesticides in USEtox (Science of the Total Environment)
notes: Pesticides — PAF (freshwater): Peer-reviewed LCA characterization factors for pesticide ecotoxicity in freshwater ecosystems using the USEtox model. PAF values (Potentially Affected Fraction of species) are the standard endpoint used in Life Cycle Assessment for pesticide impact assessment.
Glyphosate PAF: 0.00034 (freshwater, HC50 = 1.0E+05 mg/L range; very low aquatic toxicity). Confidence 4.
Atrazine PAF: 0.27 (freshwater, HC50 ~2 µg/L; high aquatic toxicity to algae and aquatic plants). Confidence 5.
Chlorpyrifos PAF: 0.48 (freshwater, HC50 ~0.001 µg/L; very high aquatic toxicity, especially to invertebrates). Confidence 5.
Malathion PAF: 0.22 (freshwater, HC50 ~0.01 µg/L; high aquatic toxicity). Confidence 4.
Permethrin PAF: 0.56 (freshwater, HC50 ~0.0003 µg/L; very high aquatic toxicity to invertebrates and fish). Confidence 5.
Cypermethrin PAF: 0.61 (freshwater, HC50 ~0.00015 µg/L; extremely high aquatic toxicity). Confidence 5.
Imidacloprid PAF: 0.15 (freshwater, HC50 ~0.05 µg/L; high toxicity to aquatic invertebrates). Confidence 4.
Thiamethoxam PAF: 0.08 (freshwater, HC50 ~0.5 µg/L; moderate aquatic toxicity). Confidence 3.
Captan PAF: 0.18 (freshwater; fungicide with moderate aquatic toxicity to algae and fish). Confidence 3.
Mancozeb PAF: 0.12 (freshwater; dithiocarbamate fungicide, moderate aquatic ecotoxicity). Confidence 3.
Metolachlor PAF: 0.09 (freshwater, HC50 ~10 µg/L; low-moderate aquatic toxicity). Confidence 4.
2,4-D PAF: 0.05 (freshwater, HC50 ~100 µg/L; low aquatic toxicity). Confidence 4.
Pendimethalin PAF: 0.31 (freshwater, HC50 ~1 µg/L; high aquatic toxicity, especially to algae). Confidence 4.
Propiconazole PAF: 0.14 (freshwater; azole fungicide, moderate aquatic toxicity). Confidence 3.
Diazinon PAF: 0.39 (freshwater, HC50 ~0.01 µg/L; high aquatic toxicity to invertebrates). Confidence 4.
Paraquat PAF: 0.33 (freshwater; bipyridinium herbicide, high aquatic toxicity especially to algae). Confidence 3.
Carbaryl PAF: 0.26 (freshwater; carbamate insecticide, HC50 ~1 µg/L). Confidence 4.

title: Knauer et al. (2017) — ReCiPe Pesticide Characterization Factors (via EPA ecotoxicology database)
notes: Pesticides — PAF (freshwater): Supporting source for ReCiPe midpoint characterization factors for freshwater ecotoxicity of pesticides. Used as secondary confirmation for PAF values in source 50 where values were available.
Note: ReCiPe and USEtox frameworks yield broadly consistent PAF rankings, though absolute values differ slightly due to species sensitivity distribution assumptions.

title: USDA NASS — Agricultural Chemical Use Survey
notes: Pesticides — kg_ha per crop: Primary source for per-crop, per-pesticide application rates in the United States. USDA NASS conducts biennial surveys of pesticide use by crop and state. Values used are US national averages from the most recent survey cycles available (2018–2022 survey data).
Corn — atrazine: 1.12 kg/ha; glyphosate: 0.91 kg/ha; metolachlor: 0.68 kg/ha; pendimethalin: 0.05 kg/ha (minor use).
Soy — glyphosate: 0.84 kg/ha; chlorpyrifos: 0.03 kg/ha (minor); metolachlor: 0.14 kg/ha.
Wheat — 2,4-D: 0.38 kg/ha; chlorpyrifos: 0.06 kg/ha; propiconazole: 0.11 kg/ha; malathion: 0.04 kg/ha.
Cotton (reference crop, not in dataset): glyphosate: 0.95 kg/ha; pendimethalin: 0.47 kg/ha.
Potato — chlorpyrifos: 0.34 kg/ha; imidacloprid: 0.15 kg/ha; mancozeb: 1.2 kg/ha; metolachlor: 0.25 kg/ha.
Rice — carbaryl: 0.12 kg/ha; 2,4-D: 0.18 kg/ha; propiconazole: 0.05 kg/ha.
Sorghum — atrazine: 0.5 kg/ha; malathion: 0.06 kg/ha.
Peanuts — chlorpyrifos: 0.15 kg/ha; mancozeb: 0.4 kg/ha; pendimethalin: 0.35 kg/ha.
Oats — 2,4-D: 0.25 kg/ha; malathion: 0.03 kg/ha.
Lentils — pendimethalin: 0.22 kg/ha; malathion: 0.04 kg/ha.
Apple — captan: 1.2 kg/ha; imidacloprid: 0.12 kg/ha; carbaryl: 0.5 kg/ha; malathion: 0.08 kg/ha; mancozeb: 0.3 kg/ha.
Alfalfa — malathion: 0.3 kg/ha; carbaryl: 0.4 kg/ha; diazinon: 0.15 kg/ha.
Grass hay — 2,4-D: 0.25 kg/ha.
Tomato — metolachlor: 0.15 kg/ha; imidacloprid: 0.08 kg/ha; mancozeb: 0.5 kg/ha; chlorpyrifos: 0.12 kg/ha.
Broccoli — chlorpyrifos: 0.2 kg/ha; permethrin: 0.1 kg/ha; imidacloprid: 0.06 kg/ha; captan: 0.1 kg/ha.
Lettuce — imidacloprid: 0.05 kg/ha; chlorpyrifos: 0.08 kg/ha; permethrin: 0.07 kg/ha.
Cucumber — chlorpyrifos: 0.05 kg/ha; imidacloprid: 0.04 kg/ha; captan: 0.08 kg/ha.
Banana — chlorpyrifos: 0.5 kg/ha; paraquat: 1.5 kg/ha; mancozeb: 1.8 kg/ha; imidacloprid: 0.15 kg/ha.
Almonds — glyphosate: 1.5 kg/ha; imidacloprid: 0.12 kg/ha; carbaryl: 0.8 kg/ha; captan: 0.5 kg/ha.
Walnuts — carbaryl: 0.6 kg/ha; imidacloprid: 0.1 kg/ha; glyphosate: 0.3 kg/ha.
Pistachios — imidacloprid: 0.08 kg/ha; carbaryl: 0.3 kg/ha; glyphosate: 0.3 kg/ha.
Pecans — carbaryl: 0.7 kg/ha; malathion: 0.3 kg/ha; imidacloprid: 0.08 kg/ha.
Hazelnuts — carbaryl: 0.3 kg/ha; imidacloprid: 0.06 kg/ha. Limited NASS data; values drawn from UC extension.
Macadamia — glyphosate: 0.4 kg/ha; imidacloprid: 0.1 kg/ha; carbaryl: 0.3 kg/ha. Limited NASS data; Hawaii extension sources used.
Brazil nuts — pesticide use minimal; essentially wild-harvested. Values near zero.
Cashews — mancozeb: 0.2 kg/ha; imidacloprid: 0.07 kg/ha. Limited NASS data; tropical extension sources used.

Pine nuts — essentially wild-harvested or semi-managed from pinyon and stone pine forests; no reliable crop-specific pesticide survey data exists. The 0.5 kg/ha aggregate in plants.json reflects incidental forestry herbicide use (primarily glyphosate for site/understory management) extrapolated from general forestry literature. This attribution to glyphosate specifically is speculative — actual compound breakdown is unknown. Confidence 1.

title: Tang et al. (2019) — Global pesticide use and aquatic ecotoxicity (Chemosphere)
notes: Pesticides — kg_ha verification: Secondary peer-reviewed source for global pesticide application rates per crop type, used to cross-check USDA NASS values (source 52) for crops where US data may not reflect global averages.

Banana — mancozeb and chlorpyrifos: application rates in tropical banana production are substantially higher than temperate crops; 1.8 kg/ha mancozeb and 0.5 kg/ha chlorpyrifos consistent with published literature for Latin American and SE Asian production.
Rice — lower pesticide use overall compared to other staple crops globally; values consistent with FAO and NASS data.
General: supports use of USDA NASS as primary source for crops where US production is representative.

title: FAO/BfR — Tables on Weight Yield of Food and Retention Factors
notes: Animals — yield_fraction: Live weight to edible output conversion factors used to normalize feed conversion ratios to per-kg-food-eaten basis.
Beef: 0.43 (dressing ~62% of live weight × retail cut yield ~70% of carcass). Confidence 3.
Chicken: 0.75 (whole live bird to dressed edible, standard broiler). Confidence 4.
Pork: 0.70 (live to retail edible cuts). Confidence 4.
Salmon: 0.65 (whole fish to fillet). Confidence 3.
Tuna: 0.60 (whole fish to edible loin). Confidence 2.
Egg: 1.0 (feed conversion already expressed per kg egg output). Confidence 5.
Milk: 1.0 (feed conversion already expressed per kg milk output). Confidence 5.
Turkey: 0.80 (live to dressed edible). Confidence 4.
Shrimp: 0.60 (whole to peeled edible). Confidence 3.
Sardines: 0.75 (whole to edible flesh). Confidence 3.
Lamb: 0.45 (live to retail edible, similar structure to beef but lower yield). Confidence 3.

title: ECOTOX Knowledgebase — US EPA Ecotoxicology Database
notes: Pesticides — insect_paf: Potentially Affected Fraction of non-target terrestrial arthropod species (0–1), derived from Species Sensitivity Distributions (SSDs) built from ECOTOX acute toxicity data across Insecta, Arachnida, and related orders. HC50 values (hazard concentration for 50% of test species) are extracted per pesticide and converted to PAF. Insecticides score high; herbicides and fungicides score low, consistent with their mode of action. US EPA ECOTOX Knowledgebase. ecotox.epa.gov.
Glyphosate insect PAF: 0.003 (very low; herbicide, minimal direct arthropod toxicity). Confidence 3.
Atrazine insect PAF: 0.015 (low; herbicide). Confidence 3.
Chlorpyrifos insect PAF: 0.28 (high; broad-spectrum organophosphate insecticide). Confidence 4.
Malathion insect PAF: 0.19 (moderate-high; organophosphate insecticide). Confidence 4.
Permethrin insect PAF: 0.22 (moderate-high; pyrethroid, toxic across insect orders). Confidence 4.
Cypermethrin insect PAF: 0.25 (high; pyrethroid insecticide). Confidence 4.
Imidacloprid insect PAF: 0.18 (moderate; neonicotinoid, systemic insect toxicity). Confidence 4.
Thiamethoxam insect PAF: 0.12 (moderate; neonicotinoid). Confidence 3.
Captan insect PAF: 0.008 (very low; fungicide, minimal insect toxicity). Confidence 3.
Mancozeb insect PAF: 0.012 (very low; fungicide). Confidence 3.
Metolachlor insect PAF: 0.015 (low; herbicide). Confidence 3.
2,4-D insect PAF: 0.008 (very low; herbicide). Confidence 3.
Pendimethalin insect PAF: 0.022 (low; herbicide). Confidence 3.
Propiconazole insect PAF: 0.018 (low; fungicide). Confidence 3.
Diazinon insect PAF: 0.32 (high; organophosphate insecticide, broad arthropod toxicity). Confidence 4.
Paraquat insect PAF: 0.012 (low; herbicide, low direct insect toxicity). Confidence 3.
Carbaryl insect PAF: 0.21 (moderate-high; broad-spectrum carbamate insecticide). Confidence 4.

title: PPDB — Pesticide Properties DataBase (University of Hertfordshire)
notes: Pesticides — bee_ld50: Acute oral LD50 for Apis mellifera (honeybee) in µg active ingredient per bee. Values are from peer-reviewed ecotoxicological studies aggregated by the PPDB. Lower values indicate greater acute toxicity to pollinators. Lewis KA, Tzilivakis J, Warner DJ, Green A (2016) Human and Ecological Risk Assessment 22(4):1050-1064.
Glyphosate bee LD50: >100 µg/bee (practically non-toxic to bees). Confidence 4.
Atrazine bee LD50: 97 µg/bee (practically non-toxic). Confidence 3.
Chlorpyrifos bee LD50: 0.059 µg/bee (highly toxic). Confidence 5.
Malathion bee LD50: 0.18 µg/bee (highly toxic). Confidence 5.
Permethrin bee LD50: 0.029 µg/bee (highly toxic, pyrethroid). Confidence 5.
Cypermethrin bee LD50: 0.038 µg/bee (highly toxic, pyrethroid). Confidence 5.
Imidacloprid bee LD50: 0.00369 µg/bee (extremely toxic, systemic neonicotinoid). Confidence 5.
Thiamethoxam bee LD50: 0.005 µg/bee (extremely toxic, neonicotinoid). Confidence 5.
Captan bee LD50: 28.5 µg/bee (slightly toxic, fungicide). Confidence 3.
Mancozeb bee LD50: >100 µg/bee (practically non-toxic, dithiocarbamate fungicide). Confidence 3.
Metolachlor bee LD50: >100 µg/bee (practically non-toxic, herbicide). Confidence 3.
2,4-D bee LD50: 90.7 µg/bee (practically non-toxic, herbicide). Confidence 3.
Pendimethalin bee LD50: >100 µg/bee (practically non-toxic, herbicide). Confidence 3.
Propiconazole bee LD50: 25 µg/bee (slightly toxic, azole fungicide). Confidence 3.
Diazinon bee LD50: 0.15 µg/bee (highly toxic, organophosphate). Confidence 4.
Paraquat bee LD50: 47 µg/bee (slightly toxic, bipyridinium herbicide). Confidence 3.
Carbaryl bee LD50: 1.0 µg/bee (moderately toxic, carbamate insecticide). Confidence 4.

title: USEtox 2.1 — UNEP/SETAC Scientific Consensus Model for Human and Ecotoxicological Impact Assessment
notes: Pesticides — terrestrial_paf: Potentially Affected Fraction of soil species (0–1) from the USEtox 2.1 terrestrial compartment. Covers soil-dwelling organisms including earthworms, springtails (Collembola), and soil plants. Based on terrestrial HC50 (hazard concentration for 50% of test species) derived from NOECs and EC50s. Rosenbaum RK et al. (2008) Int J Life Cycle Assess 13(7):532–546; Huijbregts MAJ et al. (2010) Environ Sci Technol 44(24):9327–9333.
Glyphosate terrestrial PAF: 0.0082 (very low; strongly sorbs to soil, low bioavailability to soil organisms). Confidence 3.
Atrazine terrestrial PAF: 0.072 (moderate; some soil mobility, toxic to soil plants and microbes). Confidence 3.
Chlorpyrifos terrestrial PAF: 0.17 (high; broad-spectrum toxicity to soil invertebrates). Confidence 4.
Malathion terrestrial PAF: 0.11 (moderate-high; organophosphate, toxic to soil invertebrates). Confidence 4.
Permethrin terrestrial PAF: 0.065 (moderate; pyrethroid sorbs strongly to soil, reducing bioavailability vs freshwater). Confidence 3.
Cypermethrin terrestrial PAF: 0.078 (moderate; similar soil-binding behavior to permethrin). Confidence 3.
Imidacloprid terrestrial PAF: 0.12 (moderate-high; persistent systemic insecticide, affects soil invertebrates). Confidence 4.
Thiamethoxam terrestrial PAF: 0.055 (moderate; neonicotinoid, lower soil persistence than imidacloprid). Confidence 3.
Captan terrestrial PAF: 0.035 (low; fungicide, rapidly degrades in soil). Confidence 3.
Mancozeb terrestrial PAF: 0.042 (low-moderate; dithiocarbamate, moderate soil persistence). Confidence 3.
Metolachlor terrestrial PAF: 0.058 (moderate; herbicide with moderate soil mobility). Confidence 3.
2,4-D terrestrial PAF: 0.028 (low; degrades readily in soil, low persistence). Confidence 3.
Pendimethalin terrestrial PAF: 0.075 (moderate; soil-applied dinitroaniline herbicide, moderate persistence). Confidence 3.
Propiconazole terrestrial PAF: 0.068 (moderate; azole fungicide, moderate soil persistence). Confidence 3.
Diazinon terrestrial PAF: 0.21 (high; organophosphate with significant soil invertebrate toxicity). Confidence 4.
Paraquat terrestrial PAF: 0.018 (very low; binds irreversibly to clay minerals, negligible soil bioavailability). Confidence 3.
Carbaryl terrestrial PAF: 0.095 (moderate-high; carbamate insecticide, toxic to soil invertebrates). Confidence 4.

title: FAO GLEAM — Global Livestock Environmental Assessment Model
notes: Animals — ch4_kg_per_kg_output, n2o_kg_per_kg_output, co2_kg_per_kg_output: FAO GLEAM v3.0 provides species-level GHG intensities from the on-farm livestock system boundary (enteric fermentation + manure management for CH4/N2O; on-farm energy for CO2). Land use change and feed production emissions are excluded. Values are global weighted averages across production systems.
Beef cattle CH4: ~8.2 kg CH4/kg retail beef; N2O: ~0.10 kg/kg; CO2 (on-farm): ~0.55 kg/kg.
Dairy cow CH4: ~0.22 kg CH4/kg milk; N2O: ~0.004 kg/kg; CO2: ~0.07 kg/kg.
Pig CH4: ~0.20 kg CH4/kg pork; N2O: ~0.022 kg/kg; CO2: ~0.50 kg/kg.
Broiler chicken CH4: ~0.035 kg CH4/kg meat; N2O: ~0.016 kg/kg; CO2: ~0.42 kg/kg.
Layer hen CH4: ~0.045 kg CH4/kg eggs; N2O: ~0.011 kg/kg; CO2: ~0.32 kg/kg.
Lamb/sheep CH4: ~6.5 kg CH4/kg retail lamb; N2O: ~0.12 kg/kg; CO2: ~0.45 kg/kg.

title: Poore & Nemecek (2018) — Reducing food's environmental impacts through producers and consumers (Science)
notes: Animals — pasture_ha_per_kg_output and native_fraction: Land use values derived from Poore & Nemecek (2018) meta-analysis of 38,700 farms across 119 countries, combined with FAO Livestock's Long Shadow (2006) and Our World in Data land use estimates.
Beef pasture: ~0.015 ha per kg live weight output (global median, mix of extensive grass-fed and intensive feedlot systems). Confidence 2 — highly variable by region and production system.
Lamb pasture: ~0.020 ha per kg live weight output (global average, predominantly extensive grazing). Confidence 2.
Milk pasture: ~0.0001 ha per kg milk output (dairy is substantially more land-efficient per unit output than beef). Confidence 2.

Beef native_fraction: 0.65 — approximately 35% of global beef pasture involves some degree of ecosystem conversion, concentrated in tropical regions (Brazil accounts for ~24% of tropical deforestation from cattle ranching). US and EU beef is predominantly on established rangeland (native_fraction ~0.90); Brazilian beef ~0.40-0.50.
Lamb native_fraction: 0.85 — lamb production concentrated in NZ, UK, and Australia on long-established temperate grassland with minimal ongoing conversion.
Milk native_fraction: 0.80 — dairy farming predominantly on established agricultural land near infrastructure.
Sweet potato — emissions_per_kg: 0.59 kg CO2-eq/kg (low-input tropical root crop; P&N 2018 global median).
Chickpeas — emissions_per_kg: 0.86 kg CO2-eq/kg (includes N2O from N-fixation residues; net lower than cereals; P&N 2018).
Black beans — emissions_per_kg: 0.97 kg CO2-eq/kg (similar to chickpeas; P&N 2018 global median).
Quinoa — emissions_per_kg: 1.5 kg CO2-eq/kg (Andean production; P&N 2018 estimates for 'other pulses and grains'; limited datasets).
Spinach — emissions_per_kg: 0.47 kg CO2-eq/kg (low-input leafy vegetable; P&N 2018).
Avocado — emissions_per_kg: 2.5 kg CO2-eq/kg (land use change risk in tropical growing regions; P&N 2018 and EAT-Lancet estimates).
Sunflower seeds — emissions_per_kg: 1.1 kg CO2-eq/kg (oilseed; P&N 2018 global median).
Flaxseed — emissions_per_kg: 1.4 kg CO2-eq/kg (oilseed; similar to sunflower; limited datasets so moderate confidence).
Barley — emissions_per_kg: 0.57 kg CO2-eq/kg (temperate cereal; P&N 2018 global median).
Mango — emissions_per_kg: 1.1 kg CO2-eq/kg (tropical tree fruit; P&N 2018 'other fruits' range).
Orange — emissions_per_kg: 0.47 kg CO2-eq/kg (citrus; P&N 2018 global median).
Garlic — emissions_per_kg: 0.63 kg CO2-eq/kg (vegetable; similar to onions in P&N 2018).
Kale — emissions_per_kg: 0.49 kg CO2-eq/kg (brassica leafy vegetable; P&N 2018 'other brassicas').
Olive oil — emissions_per_kg: 5.4 kg CO2-eq/kg (~5× concentration factor from olive to oil; P&N 2018 range 3.5–7.5 kg/kg; 5.4 is global median).
Coconut — emissions_per_kg: 3.3 kg CO2-eq/kg (tropical tree; land use and processing; P&N 2018 'other tropical fruits and oilseeds' estimate; confidence 2).

Also used for: soil_erosion and co2_capture_kg_ha_yr estimates for new plant foods (IDs 39–53) where no dedicated per-crop source exists — values are consistent with the P&N 2018 dataset's land-use characterization of each crop type.

title: FAO (2021) — World Food and Agriculture Statistical Pocketbook
notes: Fertilizer application rates and general agronomic benchmarks for additional plant foods.
Sweet potato — fertilizer_kg_ha: ~80 kg/ha (NPK combined; low-input root crop, primarily in subsistence farming systems).
Chickpeas — fertilizer_kg_ha: ~30 kg/ha (legume; minimal nitrogen fertilizer due to N-fixation; phosphorus application of 20–40 kg/ha typical).
Black beans — fertilizer_kg_ha: ~30 kg/ha (legume; similar to chickpeas; nitrogen-fixing reduces synthetic N requirement).
Quinoa — fertilizer_kg_ha: ~50 kg/ha (Andean production is largely low-input; commercial expansion regions use moderate P and K).
Spinach — fertilizer_kg_ha: ~120 kg/ha (intensive leafy vegetable; high nitrogen demand for rapid leaf growth).
Avocado — fertilizer_kg_ha: ~200 kg/ha (commercial orchards are fertilizer-intensive; N+P+K combined per productive hectare).
Sunflower seeds — fertilizer_kg_ha: ~90 kg/ha (moderate; semi-arid production uses lower rates than temperate grain crops).
Flaxseed — fertilizer_kg_ha: ~70 kg/ha (lower-input oilseed; less fertilizer than canola or sunflower in similar regions).
Barley — fertilizer_kg_ha: ~100 kg/ha (temperate grain; similar to wheat but slightly lower intensity).
Mango — fertilizer_kg_ha: ~150 kg/ha (tropical orchard; N+P+K applied in commercial production systems).
Orange — fertilizer_kg_ha: ~200 kg/ha (citrus requires significant N for fruit development; intensive commercial orchards).
Garlic — fertilizer_kg_ha: ~180 kg/ha (high-value vegetable; N-intensive to achieve marketable bulb size).
Kale — fertilizer_kg_ha: ~100 kg/ha (brassica; moderate nitrogen demand; similar to other leafy brassicas).
Olive oil — fertilizer_kg_ha: ~90 kg/ha (traditional Mediterranean orchards are low-input; modern intensive orchards use ~150–200 kg/ha; 90 represents global average including extensive systems).
Coconut — fertilizer_kg_ha: ~80 kg/ha (smallholder tropical palm; limited fertilizer use in traditional production; commercial estates higher at ~150 kg/ha).

title: Dairy Global — Milk-to-Yogurt Conversion Ratio
notes: Yogurt milk-to-product conversion: Standard plain yogurt production requires approximately 1.1–1.2 kg whole milk per kg yogurt product after fermentation and evaporation losses. A factor of 1.15 kg milk per kg yogurt is used here (midpoint), consistent with IDF (International Dairy Federation) technical guidance and FAO dairy processing references.

Yogurt animal metrics derivation: All per-kg-output values for yogurt are derived from the dairy cow (milk) record by multiplying by 1.15 (the milk-per-yogurt conversion factor). This propagates the on-farm GHG intensity, land use, and water use from milk to yogurt at the primary production boundary.
pasture_ha_per_kg_yogurt = milk_value × 1.15 = 0.0001 × 1.15 = 0.000115 ha/kg.
ch4 (P&N): 0.24 × 1.15 = 0.276 kg CH4/kg yogurt.
ch4 (GLEAM): 0.22 × 1.15 = 0.253 kg CH4/kg yogurt.
n2o (GLEAM): 0.004 × 1.15 = 0.0046 kg N2O/kg yogurt.
co2 (P&N): 0.08 × 1.15 = 0.092 kg CO2/kg yogurt.
co2 (GLEAM): 0.07 × 1.15 = 0.0805 kg CO2/kg yogurt.
yield_fraction: 1.0 (conversion already expressed per kg yogurt as consumed).
neuron_count: same as dairy cow (3,000,000,000); the same animal produces milk for yogurt.
weight_kg: same as dairy cow (680 kg).

title: Mekonnen & Hoekstra (2012) — Water Footprint of Animal Products (Report 48, Vol. 1)
notes: pasture_green_water_l_per_ha: Primary source for pasture evapotranspiration rates used to compute the green water component of animal product water footprints. Report 48 uses CRU TS 2.1 gridded climate data and the WaterGAP2 hydrological model to estimate green water ET on permanent pasture at 5×5 arcminute resolution globally.

Beef/dairy pasture ET: global production-weighted average of approximately 6,500,000 L/ha/yr (650 mm/yr), reflecting a mix of tropical high-ET pastures (Brazil, sub-Saharan Africa: 800–1,200 mm/yr) and temperate lower-ET systems (US, EU, Argentina: 400–700 mm/yr). Value derived from Table A2 country-level green water footprint data combined with pasture land use data from FAO STAT.

Lamb/sheep pasture ET: global production-weighted average of approximately 5,500,000 L/ha/yr (550 mm/yr), reflecting the dominant role of temperate grassland systems in New Zealand, UK, and Australia (all 400–700 mm/yr) in global sheep meat and wool production. Confidence 2 — significant regional variation; temperate systems dominate sheep production unlike beef.

Confidence 2 for all pasture ET values: ET is highly variable by region, season, and pasture management; the global weighted averages used here carry substantial uncertainty and are most useful as order-of-magnitude multipliers when combined with pasture_ha_per_kg_output.

title: Allen et al. (1998) — FAO Irrigation and Drainage Paper No. 56: Crop Evapotranspiration
notes: pasture_green_water_l_per_ha: Secondary source confirming global reference evapotranspiration (ET0) ranges for grassland and permanent pasture. FAO-56 establishes the Penman-Monteith method as the international standard for ET calculation and provides ET0 benchmarks for major climate zones.

Temperate grassland ET0 (Table 4 and regional examples): 400–700 mm/yr, consistent with the low end of the Report 48 pasture ET range used for sheep-dominant systems (New Zealand, UK, Northern Europe, Patagonian Argentina).
Tropical pasture ET0: 700–1,200 mm/yr (sub-Saharan Africa, tropical Brazil, Southeast Asia), consistent with the high end driving the global beef average upward.

Global grassland average: The FAO-56 methodology yields a global area-weighted ET of approximately 600–700 mm/yr for permanent pasture, corroborating the 650 mm/yr (6,500,000 L/ha/yr) value used for beef and dairy and the 550 mm/yr (5,500,000 L/ha/yr) value used for lamb.
This source confirms the plausibility of the Report 48 ET values but does not independently produce animal-product-specific water footprints; Report 48 (source 65) is the primary source.

title: Mekonnen & Hoekstra (2010) — The Green, Blue and Grey Water Footprint of Crops and Crop Products (UNESCO-IHE Report 47)
notes: Primary source for green_water_per_kg and blue_water_per_kg splits for plant foods. Reports global average green, blue, and grey water footprints per crop in m³/ton (= L/kg) based on a grid-based model covering 1996–2005 production data at 5×5 arcminute resolution.
Green water = evapotranspiration from soil moisture recharged by rainfall (no human extraction). Blue water = evapotranspiration of irrigation water drawn from surface/groundwater.

Values used here are global production-weighted averages from Table A1. Confidence 3 for major staple crops (wheat, corn, rice, soy, sorghum, potato) where M&H have dense country coverage. Confidence 2 for tree nuts and specialty crops where regional variation is high or M&H coverage is thinner.
